retrieved_docs.title: Central Sleep Apnea -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The goal standard for diagnosis is a sleep study called polysomnogram that measures breathing effort and airflow, vitals, and blood oxygen level during different stages of sleep. Treatment includes identifying underlying causes and treating any precipitating factors. Ideally, these patients need to be evaluated by a sleep physician.

retrieved_docs.content: For patients with OSA, positive pressure therapy is the mainstay of treatment. However, additional treatments are available for those who fail positive pressure therapy or have an inadequate response to positive pressure therapy alone.

retrieved_docs.title: Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy -- Treatment Planning -- Systemic Risk Factor Management
retrieved_docs.content: Blood pressure: Nocturnal hypotension should be avoided by reviewing antihypertensive medications and adjusting dosing to prevent reduced perfusion during sleep. Diabetes and glycemic control: Tight regulation reduces endothelial dysfunction and ischemic burden. Dyslipidemia: Statins should be initiated if low-density lipoprotein >100 mg/dL according to the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association guidelines. Smoking cessation: Smoking cessation is strongly advised due to vasoconstrictive and atherosclerotic effects. Sleep apnea evaluation: Polysomnography is indicated for patients with snoring, obesity, or excessive daytime sleepiness. CPAP therapy improves vascular health and may reduce the risk of recurrence. [95]

retrieved_docs.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea -- Treatment / Management -- Lifestyle Changes and Treating Underlying Medical Conditions
retrieved_docs.content: The importance of weight loss should be emphasized in patients with OSA who are overweight or have obesity. [53] [54] Although weight loss is recommended and can often decrease the severity of OSA, it is usually not curative. Patients should be educated about the impact of sleep duration on their health and encouraged to prioritize getting at least 7 to 8 hours of sleep each night. [55]

retrieved_docs.title: Central Sleep Apnea -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Nocturnal oxygen therapy in previous trials has decreased the number of apneic episodes during sleep times for patients with CHF. It also improved NYHA functional class quality of life, and EF was noted at the end of 12-week in patients with central sleep apnea. [28] These findings were confirmed over 52 weeks in a similar trial, ensuring improved quality of life. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Sleep and Neurodegenerative Disorders -- Treatment / Management -- Obstructive Sleep Apnea
retrieved_docs.content: Patient education and behavior modification, such as controlling obesity, avoiding alcohol, and sedating medications, are recommended. An apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) >15 events/hour warrants positive airway pressure (PAP) therapy. [79] PAP therapy is also recommended for patients with mild OSA (AHI of 5 to 15) who have excessive daytime sleepiness. [80]

retrieved_docs.title: COPD and Sleep Apnea Overlap -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Overlap syndrome has a higher rate of morbidity and mortality than either condition alone. Therefore, concurrent management of both disorders is imperative to achieve optimal nocturnal oxygen saturation, prevent multiple arousals, and improve quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Sleep Apnea Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Because OSA is so prevalent and has numerous negative health-related outcomes, physicians must appropriately evaluate and screen patients for symptoms and signs of OSA. When diagnosed, patients should be educated regarding the pathophysiology, expected course, and treatment options, including lifestyle modifications, positive pressure therapy, and alternative/additional therapies as indicated. They should also be counseled regarding the need for long-term follow-up and monitoring for potential complications of OSA.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1975
retrieved_docs.content: Fenofibrate (fenofibrát) is contraindicated in patients with renal failure (قصور الكلى), meaning its use should be avoided in individuals whose kidney function is impaired.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2156
retrieved_docs.content: Increased anion gap is clinically associated with renal failure, indicating that patients with renal failure often exhibit a widened anion gap as part of their metabolic disturbance. Because renal failure impairs drug clearance, the lipid‑lowering agent fenofibrate is contraindicated in this condition, and clinicians should avoid prescribing fenofibrate to patients with renal failure. Recognizing the link between anion‑gap elevation and renal failure guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and informs safe medication choices in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1977
retrieved_docs.content: Klofibrát produces a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, thereby influencing lipid metabolism, but it is contraindicated in patients with تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic steatosis). Consequently, clinicians should consider the lipid‑lowering benefit of Klofibrát while avoiding its use in individuals with hepatic steatosis to prevent potential liver‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2741
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain constitute a class of lipid‑modifying agents that are used to improve abnormal blood lipid profiles, particularly by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly raising high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol. The primary members of this plain fibrate class are bezafibrát, klofibrát, and fenofibrát. All three agents act as agonists of peroxisome proliferator‑activated receptor‑α (PPAR‑α), leading to increased oxidation of fatty acids, reduced hepatic production of very‑low‑density lipoprotein, and enhanced clearance of triglyceride‑rich lipoproteins. Because of these mechanisms, bezafibrát, klofibrát, and fenofibrát are employed in the management of hypertriglyceridaemia, mixed dyslipidaemia, and certain cases of secondary dyslipidaemia, contributing to cardiovascular risk reduction when used appropriately in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1978
retrieved_docs.content: Bezafibrate is classified as a member of the fibrates, a group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As part of this drug class, bezafibrate shares the typical pharmacologic actions of fibrates, which are used to alter lipid profiles by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2467
retrieved_docs.content: Clofibrate (klofrolát) exerts a physiologic effect by decreasing lipolysis, which contributes to its lipid‑lowering action. Because of its pharmacologic profile, clofibrate is contraindicated in patients with renal failure (قصور الكلى), and should be avoided in this population to prevent adverse outcomes.

retrieved_docs.content: Bezafibrate and fenofibrate are both classified as fibrates, a group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. Their shared classification underscores their similar pharmacologic actions and therapeutic role in cardiovascular risk reduction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 778
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain constitute a class of lipid‑modifying agents that includes the individual drugs bezafibrát, klofibrát, and fenofibrát. Each of these agents is a member of the fibrate class and functions to alter lipid metabolism. Specifically, klofibrát exerts a physiologic effect characterized by decreased lipolysis, contributing to its overall lipid‑lowering activity. Together, these fibrate medications are employed to manage dyslipidaemia by influencing the breakdown and synthesis of lipids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 127
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain constitute a therapeutic class of lipid‑lowering agents that includes the individual drugs bezafibrát, klofibrát, fenofibrát and gemfibrozil. Each of these agents is classified as a member of the fibrate group, reflecting their shared mechanism of reducing plasma triglycerides and modulating lipoprotein metabolism. Among them, klofibrát exerts a physiologic effect characterized by decreased lipolysis, which contributes to its lipid‑lowering action. However, klofibrát is contraindicated in the presence of تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic congestion), indicating that patients with this hepatic condition should avoid its use. Gemfibrozil is further linked to the formulation category G [Preparations], of which it is a parent compound, underscoring its inclusion in a specific preparation subgroup. Collectively, these relationships delineate the classification, pharmacologic effects, and safety considerations pertinent to fibrate therapy in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
